coredata.dc:title: Efficient degradation of Levofloxacin with magnetically separable ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst: Vis-NIR light response ability and mechanism insight

Niu, Cheng-Gang
Guo, Hai

Ruan, Min
Zhang, Lei
Liang, Chao
Liu, Hui-Yun
Yang, Ya-Ya
coredata.dc:description: A highly efficient and easily recoverable catalyst is always desirable for practical photocatalytic applications. In this study, a magnetically separable ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst using nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs) as solid-state electron mediators was fabricated by an easy-operated deposition-precipitation method. The prepared ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 photocatalyst exhibits a promoted photocatalytic activity for Levofloxacin (LVFX) removal compared to single ZnFe2O4 and Ag2CO3, achieving 88.75% and 22.53% degradation efficiency under visible and near-infrared (NIR) light irradiation, respectively. Characterization results show that the synergistic effect of remarkable charge carrier separation efficiency and broadened solar light absorption is responsible for the enhanced photocatalytic performance. The well-matched band structures of the two semiconductors facilitate the charge transfer in Z-scheme transfer model, resulting in high charge separation rate. Simultaneously, due to the superb electron transfer character of NCDs, the introduction of NCDs can further accelerate the transfer of the photo-generated charges as solid-state electron mediators between ZnFe2O4 and Ag2CO3. Furthermore, the unique up-converted photoluminescence properties of NCDs can broaden the optical absorption of the prepared ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 to NIR region, thus leading to an enhanced utilization of solar energy. This work provides a novel insight into the fabrication of novel all-solid-state Z-scheme photocatalyst based on nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots and opens new vista for the design of magnetically separable and highly efficient photocatalysts for environmental remediation.

head.ce:title: Efficient degradation of Levofloxacin with magnetically separable ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst: Vis-NIR light response ability and mechanism insight

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A highly efficient and easily recoverable catalyst is always desirable for practical photocatalytic applications. In this study, a magnetically separable ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst using nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs) as solid-state electron mediators was fabricated by an easy-operated deposition-precipitation method. The prepared ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 photocatalyst exhibits a promoted photocatalytic activity for Levofloxacin (LVFX) removal compared to single ZnFe2O4 and Ag2CO3, achieving 88.75% and 22.53% degradation efficiency under visible and near-infrared (NIR) light irradiation, respectively. Characterization results show that the synergistic effect of remarkable charge carrier separation efficiency and broadened solar light absorption is responsible for the enhanced photocatalytic performance. The well-matched band structures of the two semiconductors facilitate the charge transfer in Z-scheme transfer model, resulting in high charge separation rate. Simultaneously, due to the superb electron transfer character of NCDs, the introduction of NCDs can further accelerate the transfer of the photo-generated charges as solid-state electron mediators between ZnFe2O4 and Ag2CO3. Furthermore, the unique up-converted photoluminescence properties of NCDs can broaden the optical absorption of the prepared ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 to NIR region, thus leading to an enhanced utilization of solar energy. This work provides a novel insight into the fabrication of novel all-solid-state Z-scheme photocatalyst based on nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots and opens new vista for the design of magnetically separable and highly efficient photocatalysts for environmental remediation.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, antibiotics have been wildly employed to the treatment of bacterial infection due to their excellent antibacterial effect [1,2]. Levofloxacin (LVFX), possessing broad-spectrum antimicrobial properties and strong antibacterial, has been listed as one of the most commonly used antibiotics [3,4]. Frustratingly, the overuse of antibiotics results in their unexpected accumulation in aquatic environment, thus posing a serious threat on human health and aquatic ecosystems [5–7]. So far, many technologies have been put forward for the control of antibiotics in aquatic environment, such as photocatalysis [8], advanced oxidation process [9,10] and adsorption [11]. Among them, semiconductor-based photocatalysis has been considered to be one of the most appealing method due to its low-energy, environmental-friendly, and reusable properties. In addition, this technology has been put into widespread use in recent years for H2 evolution, CO2 reduction and organic contaminants degradation [12–15]. And the photocatalytic degradation for LVFX has been also investigated by many researchers. For example, Adhikari Sangeeta and co-authors achieved high-efficiency photocatalytic degradation of LVFX by using MoS2/Ag2Mo2O7 as photocatalyst, and the related photocatalytic mechanism for LVFX removal was also proposed [2,16,17]. However, the traditional photocatalysts are difficult to be separated from liquid phase, thus bringing secondary pollution to the aquatic environment, which is one of the primary problems in practical application process [13]. Now that the magnetic substrates exhibits remarkable recyclability under external magnetic field, the investigations on magnetically separable photocatalyst show great potential in the field of photocatalysis [18].

Spinel zinc ferrite (ZnFe2O4), a magnetic semiconductor with a relatively narrow band gap of 1.87 eV, has spawned ever growing interests in photocatalysis due to its excellent visible light response, extraordinary photochemical stability and easy recyclability [19,20]. Nonetheless, the ineffective charge separation and high recombination of photogenerated carriers severely restrict its photocatalytic performance in practical application [21]. In recent years, the design of hybrid photocatalysts with the construction of a heterojunction has been regarded as an effective strategy for preventing the recombination of photoinduced holes and electrons, which holds a strong redox ability at spatially separate sites [22,23]. And up to now, lots of ZnFe2O4-based heterojunction composites with rapid charge separation efficiency and remarkable photocatalytic performance have been reported, such as ZnFe2O4/Ag2O [24], Bi2O2CO3/ZnFe2O4 [25], ZnFe2O4/ZnO [26] and so on. Inspired by above successful cases, combining ZnFe2O4 with other semiconductors to construct heterojunctions will be an effective method for boosting photocatalytic performance of ZnFe2O4.

Silver carbonate (Ag2CO3), with a suitable band gap of 2.3 eV, exhibits outstanding optical performance, which is usually used as a co-catalyst in photocatalyst [2]. Especially, due to its high valence band position (+2.92 eV vs. NHE), Ag2CO3 is always used to construct Z-scheme heterojunction to retain its strong oxidation potential and promote the transfer of photoinduced charges. For instance, Liu and coworkers synthesized a Z-scheme photocatalyst with extraordinary photocatalytic performance and good stability by modifying g-C3N4 with Ag2CO3 and graphene oxide [27]. Similarly, Wen et al. also found that with the decoration of Ag2CO3 and AgBr, the ternary AgBr/CeO2/Ag2CO3 formed a unique double Z-scheme system, thereby holding superior photocatalytic activity [2]. Considering the advantage of Ag2CO3 and well-matched band positions between ZnFe2O4 and Ag2CO3, it is rational to use Ag2CO3 as a co-catalyst to accelerate the separation rate of photoinduced charges of ZnFe2O4 with the construction of a Z-scheme heterojunction.

Over the past decade, the Z-scheme charge transfer mechanism has been put forward as a new charge transfer type and has been attracted numerous attentions. Compared to conventional type-II heterojunction photocatalyst, Z-scheme photocatalyst possesses stronger redox capacity due to its maximized utilization of more positive VB from one catalyst and more negative CB from another one [28–32]. And the specific tandem configuration of the constructed Z-scheme photocatalyst provides an optimal energy to power each photocatalyst, which is beneficial for achieving efficient charge transportation and suppressing electron-hole recombination, thus enhancing the photoactivity of as-prepared composite. However, the competition between traditional charge transfer and Z-scheme charge transfer process always exists in some semiconductor heterojunctions, which will undoubtedly induce a negative impact on the photocatalytic activity [33]. Hence, introducing an electron transfer mediator between these two semiconductors, generally serving as a contact interface, is of vital significance for accelerating the transfer of photoinduced charge carries and promoting the construction of an efficient Z-scheme system [34].

Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs), a novel and ecofriendly zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial with sizes below 10 nm, have drawn lots of attention because of their excellent electric and optical properties [35]. The conjugated π structure of NCDs not only brings them superb electron reservoir and transfer properties but also enhances the contact between NCDs and other semiconductors to establish stable composites [36]. What’s more, NCDs with unique up-converted photoluminescence properties can act as photosensitizers for catching both visible and NIR light [37]. When they couple with other semiconductors, the composites can turn a NIR light into a visible light. These characteristics make NCDs an ideal candidate for modifying the semiconductors as an electron transfer mediator to improve their photocatalytic activities and the utilization of solar light. Therefore, it is anticipated that introduction of NCDs into binary ZnFe2O4/Ag2CO3 system can further promote their light response range and separation rate of photoinduced charges.

Herein, a magnetically separable ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 all-solid-state Z-scheme photocatalytic system was successfully constructed by a mild deposition-precipitation process. In this hybrid structure, ZnFe2O4 and Ag2CO3 play the roles of separating photoinduced electron-hole pairs through the formed Z-scheme heterojunction, while NCDs act as the transport bridge for photogenerated carriers and photosensitizers for light absorption. The phase, chemical composition, morphologies, photoelectric and optical properties of the prepared catalysts were investigated in detail. The photocatalytic activity of the composite under both visible and NIR light were evaluated by Levofloxacin (LVFX) degradation and the main degradation intermediates were further analyzed by high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectra (HPLC-MS). Additionally, the cycle experiment and VSM were used to research the stability and magnetic behavior of the obtained ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 photocatalyst. The radical trapping experiments and electron spin resonance (ESR) technology were carried out to detect the active species generated during reaction. In light of above experiment analysis, a plausible degradation mechanism of LVFX over ZnFe2O4/ NCDs/Ag2CO3 was proposed.

All the reagents with analytical grade are used directly in this experiment. The NCDs aqueous was obtained according to the previous reported method [38]. Firstly, 1.05 g citric acid and 0.335 mL ethylenediamine were added into 10 mL deionized water. Then, the mixture was placed into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and sealed at 200 °C for 5 h. Finally, above acquired liquid was centrifuged for removing large particles and then followed by freeze drying. The acquired NCDs solid were dissolved into deionized water to get uniform NCDs aqueous (1 mg/mL). The resultant dark yellow solution was water soluble NCDs.

Magnetic ZnFe2O4 was fabricated through a hydrothermal and calcined method [24]. In a typical process, 6 mmol Fe(NO3)3·9H2O and 3 mmol Zn(NO3)2·6H2O were dissolved in 35 mL ethylene glycol. Then 10 mL ethylene glycol solution containing 9 mmol sodium acetate was dropped into the above mixture and kept stirring for 30 min at ambient temperature. Subsequently, the suspension was heated at 180 °C in a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave for 18 h. The resulting black precursor then turned to orange by calcining at 500 °C.

ZnFe2O4/Ag2CO3 composites were obtained via a facile deposition–precipitation process. Shortly, 0.20 g ZnFe2O4 was dispersed into 60 mL deionized water with the assistance of ultrasound. Subsequently, a certain amount of 0.10 mol/L AgNO3 aqueous was added into aforementioned mixture and kept stirring for 30 min. Then, excess 0.10 mol/L NaHCO3 solution was dripped into the mixed under stirring in darkness for 2 h. The resulting precipitate was dried at 60 °C after rinsed with deionized water thoroughly. The obtained samples were denoted as ZnFe2O4/Ag2CO3-17%, ZnFe2O4/Ag2CO3-21%, ZnFe2O4/Ag2CO3-25% (or ZnFe2O4/Ag2CO3) and ZnFe2O4/Ag2CO3-29% when the mass of Ag2CO3 accounts for 17%, 21%, 25% and 29% among entire composite, respectively.

In a typical synthesis process, a certain amount of NCDs (1 mg/mL) were dropped into 5.0 mL AgNO3 solution (0.10 mol/L), after ultrasonicating for 20 min, the homogeneous liquid was added to 60 mL deionized water with 0.20 g ZnFe2O4 dissolved. Followed by 30 min stirring, excess 0.10 mol/L NaHCO3 solution was dripped into the mixed. After stirring in dark condition for 2 h, the precipitate was dried at 60 °C after rinsed with deionized water thoroughly. The as-prepared samples were denoted as ZnFe2O4/NCDs-1/Ag2CO3-25%, ZnFe2O4/NCDs-3/Ag2CO3-25%, ZnFe2O4/NCDs-5/Ag2CO3-25% (or ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3) and ZnFe2O4/NCDs-7/Ag2CO3-25% for the NCDs feeding amount of 1 mL, 3 mL, 5 mL and 7 mL, respectively.

In comparation, pure Ag2CO3 was synthesized in the absence of ZnFe2O4 and NCDs. The ZnFe2O4/NCDs-5 (or ZnFe2O4/NCDs) composite was synthesized by mixing 5 mL NCDs (1 mg/mL) with 0.20 g ZnFe2O4 in 60 mL deionized water. The Ag2CO3/NCDs-5 (or Ag2CO3/NCDs) composite was prepared by the same method as the ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 in the absence of ZnFe2O4, and the amount of NCDs were 5 mL.

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) of the prepared catalysts were performed on a Bruker D8 ADVANCE with the irradiation of Cu Kα. The morphology and crystal planes of the samples were identified by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (TEM, JEM-2010). The distributions of elements in photocatalyst were observed by energy-dispersive spectrometry (EDS). Chemical composition was investigated through the X-ray photoelectron microscopy (XPS) on Thermo ESCALAB 250XI spectrometer with Al Kα source. A Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrometer was applied to record the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area was investigated on TRISTAR-3000 analyzer. The mappings of 3D EEMs were collected from a Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer. The magnetic properties were carried out on a MPMS (SQUID) XL-7 vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM). The electron spin resonance (ESR) signals of superoxide and hydroxyl radicals were investigated on a Bruker model ESR E500 spectrometer.

The photocatalytic performances of the synthesized catalysts were assessed by the degradation of LVFX under both visible and NIR light irradiation. A 300 W Xe lamp (Zhong jiao jin yuan, CEL-HXF300) equipped with 420 nm and 760 nm cut filters was used as light source. Typically, 30 mg photocatalyst was put into LVFX (50 mL, 10 mg/L) solution. Before irradiation, the suspension was kept stirring for 30 min in darkness to achieve the equilibrium between adsorption and desorption. At a certain interval of irradiation, 3 mL suspension was collected after centrifugation remove the residual particles. The relevant concentration of residual LVFX was measured by an UV–vis spectrophotometer at its maximum absorption peak (288 nm). The UV–vis graph of different concentrations of LVFX is provided in Supporting Information (Fig. S1). The photodegradation intermediates of LVFX were investigated by LC–MS. Related details can be found in Supporting Information.

The photoelectrochemical characteristics of the as-prepared materials were investigated in a conventional three-electrode model on an electrochemical workstation (CHI760D, Shang Hai, China). Pt wire and Ag/AgCl electrodes were acted as counter and reference electrodes, respectively, while the FTO with photocatalysts coated on as the working electrode. As for the preparation the working electrodes, 10 mg of the as-prepared catalyst was put into 0.1 mL of 25% PVA solution, the suspension after sonication was put onto FTO slice containing active area of 1 cm2 and dried at 80 ℃. During the photoelectrochemical test process, 0.2 mol/L sodium sulfate electrolyte solution was used as an electrolyte and a 300 W Xe lamp (Zhong jiao jin yuan, CEL-HXF300) equipped with 420 nm and 760 nm cut filters offered the light source. The photocurrent response was measured under an open-circuit potential. To further study the photoelectrochemical characteristics of the prepared samples, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was investigated on an electrochemical workstation (CHI760D, Shang Hai, China) equipped with a frequency analyzer module in the frequency range of 105 to 10−2 Hz. Finally, Mott-Schottky was measured with a frequency of 1 kHz.

The phase purities and crystal structures of Ag2CO3, ZnFe2O4, ZnFe2O4/Ag2CO3 and ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 composites were investigated by the X-ray diffraction (XRD) technique. Just as shown in Fig. 1 , the pure Ag2CO3 peaks appear at 2θ = 18.61°, 20.60°, 32.63°, 33.70°, 37.82° and 39.62°, which can be indexed to the (0 2 0), (1 1 0), (−1 0 1), (−1 3 0), (2 0 0), and (0 3 1) planes, respectively (JCPDS No. 26-0339) [39]. Meanwhile, the six diffraction peaks of single ZnFe2O4 at 2θ = 30.1°, 35.4°, 43.1°, 53.4°, 56.9° and 62.5° (JCPDS standard card 22-1012) correspond to the indexes (2 2 0), (3 1 1), (4 0 0), (4 2 2), (5 1 1) and (4 4 0), respectively [40]. And the broad peaks reveal its small crystal size. Apparently, all the major peaks of ZnFe2O4 and Ag2CO3 are preserved in the XRD pattern of ZnFe2O4/Ag2CO3 and ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3, demonstrating that the combination with Ag2CO3 and NCDs do not affect the crystal structure of ZnFe2O4. However, there are no obvious diffraction patterns of NCDs, which may be abscribed to its low concentration and weak diffraction signal [35]. Simultaneously, no additional crystal phases are observed in all patterns, suggesting the high purity of the as-prepared catalysts.

The morphologies of the as-prepared composites were identified by TEM and HRTEM. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the pristine ZnFe2O4 exhibits a uniform spherical structure with diameters ranging from 100 nm to 150 nm, which will provide large surface area and considerable active sites for the photocatalytic degradation. As displayed in Fig. 2c and d, Ag2CO3 nanoparticles and the small-sized NCDs are well anchored on the surface of ZnFe2O4, forming a close contacted ternary composites. After the introduction of Ag2CO3 and NCDs, there are no obvious morphology change of ZnFe2O4, indicating its good structural stability. From the HRTEM images in Fig. 2d, we can find three different lattice spaces of 0.253, 0.209 and 0.33 nm, which can be assigned to (3 1 1) plane of ZnFe2O4, (1 3 1) plane of Ag2CO3 and (0 0 2) plane of NCDs, respectively. Additionally, SEM image (Fig. 2e) and corresponding EDS mapping (Fig. 2f ~ k) were performed to further investigate the appearance and element distribution of the ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 nanocomposite. We can see from Fig. 2e that there are numerous well-dispersed ZnFe2O4 nanospheres decorated with little Ag2CO3 nanoparticles, which is completely in conformity with the results of TEM images. And EDS image of ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 composite shows that six elements of Zn, Ag, O, Fe, N and C are observed in the sample, which is accord with the element composition of ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 nanocomposite. Consequently, results above not only demonstrate the successful synthesis of the ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 composite but also prove the close contact among the three phases, which contribute to the separation of the photoinduced charges and boost the ultimate photocatalytic activity.

The specific surface area and pore characteristic of the as-prepared samples were investigated by nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherm measurements. As displayed in Fig. 3 and Table S1, the BET surface area (SBET) of the ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 nanocomposite (29.5317 m2 g−1) shows a slight decrease compared to that of pure ZnFe2O4 (33.3262 m2 g−1). This may be attributed to the fact that the Ag2CO3 nanoparticles with a relatively small SBET of 3.27 m2 g−1 are dispersed on the surface of large sphere-like ZnFe2O4 substrate. Hence, the surface pore of ZnFe2O4 might be impeded by Ag2CO3 nanoparticles, resulting in a lower SBET of ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3. From the results of BET analysis, it can be inferred that the surface area is not the major factor determining the photocatalytic activity in this system.

To further investigate the surface chemical states and compositions of the as-prepared ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3, XPS analysis was carried out. It is clear in Fig. 4 , the survey XPS spectrum shows that the main elements of ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 composite are Zn, Fe, Ag, O, C and N, which is consistent with the results of EDS. Fig. 4b exhibits the high-resolution XPS spectra of Fe 2p with two main peaks at 711.3 eV and 724.2 eV, corresponding to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, respectively, which reveals that Fe element presents as Fe3+ state [41,42]. As shown in Fig. 4c, the peaks centered on 1020.9 eV and 1044.1 eV separately belong to the binding energy of Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2, suggesting the appearance of Zn2+ [43]. As for Ag 3d (Fig. 4d), there are two obvious peaks situating at binding energies of 367.8 eV and 374.1 eV, which can be ascribed to Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2. This result demonstrates Ag element presents in ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 nanocomposite in the form of Ag+ [44]. The high-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum is displayed in Fig. 3e and two evident peaks are observed at peaks of 284.8 eV and 288.3 eV. The peak at 284.8 eV can be identified as CC bond in NCDs [35]. Another peak centered on 288.3 eV belongs to CO band in Ag2CO3 [45]. The O 1s peak (Fig. 4F) can be divided into three peaks. Two main peaks located at 529.7 eV and 530.4 eV correspond to oxygen species of the lattice oxygen of ZnFe2O4 and Ag2CO3, respectively [43,46]. Another small one with the binding energy of 531.6 eV may derive from the surface hydroxyl group or the adsorbed H2O on the surface of the catalysts [47]. And the N 1s peak situated at 401.1 eV comes from the NH in the NCDs [48]. The XPS analysis further confirms the successful preparation of ZnFe2O4, Ag2CO3 and NCDs.

For exploring the optical characteristics of the as-prepared composites, UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra were conducted. As depicted in Fig. 5 a, the pristine ZnFe2O4 nanospheres present a strong absorption edge at 710 nm, originating from the photo-inspired charge transfer from its valence band to the conduction band [49]. Compared to ZnFe2O4, single Ag2CO3 exhibits a relatively low visible-light absorption in visible-light region, with an band gap edge around 530 nm [46]. The composite of ZnFe2O4/Ag2CO3 shows a mixed optical absorbance with the absorption edge located at 700 nm, indicating a strong interaction between ZnFe2O4 and Ag2CO3. After the introduction of NCDs, an obvious red shift edge is observed, suggesting enhanced optical absorption of ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 composite. The phenomenon is attributed to the uniform distribution of NCDs on the surface of ZnFe2O4 and Ag2CO3, which strengthens their visible light harvesting capacity. It’s known that NCDs show excellent upconversion photoluminescence properties according to previous reports [50]. Therefore, when NCDs couple with ZnFe2O4 and Ag2CO3, the composites can transform the near infrared wavelength light into a shorter visible light, thereby promoting the light absorbing ability of the composites. Furthermore, band gaps of pristine ZnFe2O4 and Ag2CO3 were calculated by the Kubelka-Munk function [51]:
α h υ = A
( h υ - E g )
n / 2

In this formula, α, h, ʋ, Eg, and A denotes the absorption coefficient, Planck constant, light frequency, band gap energy, and proportionality constant, respectively. In addition, n is related to the inner characteristic of semiconductor and it is 1 for pristine ZnFe2O4 and Ag2CO3 because they are both direct semiconductors. According to the (αhʋ)2 against (hʋ) curve (Fig. 5b), band gap energies of ZnFe2O4 and Ag2CO3 are 1.87 and 2.57 eV, respectively.

LVFX was utilized as target pollutants to assess the photocatalytic activities of the prepared samples under both visible and NIR light irradiation. Prior to photocatalytic reaction, the adsorption–desorption equilibrium is achieved by continuously stirring for 30 min in the dark. As displayed in Fig. 6 a, the blank test result suggests the self-degradation of LVFX is almost negligible. It is noticed that the single photocatalyst shows limited activities, which is because of undesirable charge carrier recombination. For pure ZnFe2O4, the degradation efficiency for LVFX is only 11.82%, and it is 66.52% for pristine Ag2CO3 under the same condition. However, the binary and ternary composites exhibit substantially enhanced photocatalytic performance compared to pristine photocatalyst. We carried out a series of experiments to investigate the optimal ratio of ZnFe2O4, NCDs and Ag2CO3. The relative description and data are presented in the Supporting Information (Fig. S2). Among all samples, the ternary sample, ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3, achieves the highest degradation efficiency and approximately 88.75% of LVFX can be degraded within 90 min. The enhancement for degradation ability of the ternary sample can be put down to the reasons: (i) the fabricated Z-scheme heterojunction boosts the diffusion of photoinduced charges as well as interfacial charge transfer process; (ii) the introduction of NCDs not only expedites the separation of photoinduced charge carrier as a transport medium but also brings wider light absorption.

To fully explain the degradation process, the Langmuir-Hinshelwood model was applied and the reaction rate constants of the catalysts follow the sequence as ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 > ZnFe2O4/Ag2CO3 > Ag2CO3/NCDs > Ag2CO3 > ZnFe2O4/NCDs > ZnFe2O4, which well matches with the degradation efficiency. Obviously, ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 composites achieve the highest reaction constant, 0.02081 min−1, which is 1.87, 1.71, 18.58, 12.17 and 1.40 folds as high as that of Ag2CO3, Ag2CO3/NCDs, ZnFe2O4, ZnFe2O4/NCDs and ZnFe2O4/Ag2CO3 respectively. This result further demonstrates that ZnFe2O4 couples with Ag2CO3 and NCDs can boost the separation rate of photoinduced holes and electrons, and thus promote the photocatalytic performance.

In addition, the photocatalytic performance of the synthesized ZnFe2O4, ZnFe2O4/NCDs and ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 have been further studied under the NIR light irradiation. As Fig. 7 a shown, the photoactivity of pristine ZnFe2O4 can be neglected under NIR light irradiation because of its low photon energy. Nevertheless, the photocatalytic performance of ZnFe2O4/NCDs is promoted compared to single ZnFe2O4 due to the upconverted photoluminescent properties of NCDs, which can convert NIR light to visible light so that the photocatalytic reaction system can produce efficient active species. Furthermore, the as-prepared ternary ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 composite shows the highest photocatalytic performance among all composites, presenting a 22.53% degradation efficiency of LVFX under NIR light irradiation within 180 min, which may be ascribed to the enhanced charges separation rate and the broadened solar light absorption, resulting from the synergistic effect of formed Z-scheme heterojunction as well as upconverted photoluminescent character of NCDs. Meanwhile, from the Fig. 7b, we can see that the rate constant of ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 is 0.000561 min−1, which is 11.6 and 3.92 times higher than that of pristine ZnFe2O4 and ZnFe2O4/NCDs. The results above reveal that the ternary ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 composite shows high photocatalytic activity under both visible light and NIR light irradiation.

In natural environment, the concentration of LVFX varies extensively. Therefore, the investigation for the influence of different initial LVFX concentration on photocatalyst’s performance is necessary. From Fig. 8 a, it is known that the initial concentration of LVFX has a great impact on photocatalytic degradation reaction and the degradation efficiency of LVFX is decreased from 88.75% to 45.65% as the concentration increases from 10 mg/L to 40 mg/L. Actually, intermediates would be produced in high concentration LVFX solution during photodegradation process, which are the strong competitors for LVFX molecules to get the limited active adsorption and catalytic sites. Moreover, higher initial pollutant concentrations could restrain the penetration of light into aqueous solution, resulting in the reduction of transfer path of photons and available photons [52]. Therefore, the concentration of LVFX is selected as 10 mg/L during the whole experiment for eliminating the negative effect and achieving optimal photocatalytic activity.

The optimal dosage of catalysts is of great importance for reducing industrial production cost and achieving high photocatalytic efficiency in practical application. Thus, photocatalytic activities of LVFX with different catalyst dosage in the range of 0.20–1.40 g/L were performed to find out the optimal dosage. Fig. 8b shows that the degradation efficiency of LVFX is significantly improved from 64.52% to 88.75% with the amount of ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 photocatalyst increasing from 0.20 g/L to 0.60 g/L. However, as the photocatalyst dosage further increase, the degradation efficiency of LVFX decrease gradually. The reasons of the observed phenomenon may put down to the following two. On the one hand, the number of photons is a constant under the irradiation of visible light in a certain time and the low dosage of photocatalyst results in low LVFX degradation efficiency due to the insufficient utilization of photos [53]. Therefore, there should be an optimal dosage of photocatalyst to make full use of the photo energy. On the other hand, the turbidity of solution can be increased by the increasing dosage of catalyst, which reduces light penetration and thus inhibits the photocatalytic ability. Hence, the catalyst dosage of 0.60 g/L is chosen as optimal dosage for the whole experiment.

In actual water bodies, there are many inorganic ions that can interfere with the removal of target pollutants. Research on the influence of the co-existed anions on LVFX removal in ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 system can provide theoretical basis for practical applications. Hence, typical cations such as Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+ and anions such as Cl−,
S
O
4
2-
,
N
O
3
-
were added into photodegradation system at a concentration of 5 mM to evaluate their effects on the LVFX removal. As shown in Fig. 8c, the addition of Na+ has a slight effect on photodegradation process, which can be neglected, while both Ca2+ and Mg2+ show significant inhibition for the degradation of LVFX and the addition of Mg2+ presents a more obvious negative effect on the degradation of LVFX than Ca2+. The decrease in photocatalytic performance could be explained by the fact that both Ca2+ and Mg2+ are able to form metal complexes with LVFX, making LVFX difficult to degrade [37]. In addition, the more obvious inhabitation of Mg2+ is attributed to its stronger coordination ability. As depicted in Fig. 8d, the addition of
S
O
4
2-
and
N
O
3
-
cause negligible effect on the degradation process. The presence of Cl– poses slight decrease in photodegradation process, which possibly puts down to the inevitable competitive adsorption between Cl– and LVFX at the active sites of catalyst [12].

To further investigate the photocatalytic activity of the ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 catalyst towards different contaminants, photodegradation experiments for Oxytetracycline hydrochloride (OTC), Ciprofloxacin (CIP) and Enrofloxacin (EFA) were carried out under visible light. With the irradiation time of 90 min, the degradation efficiency of ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 towards OTC (20 mg/L), CIP (10 mg/L) and EFA (10 mg/L) are 91.21%, 82.38% and 73.86%, respectively (Fig. 8f). The results reveal that the as-prepared photocatalyst exhibits remarkable photocatalytic performance for the removal of antibiotics.

LC–MS technique was used to further detect intermediates produced in photodegradation reactions, which contributes to an in-depth understanding of the mineralization pathways of LVFX. According to previous reports, the degradation pathway of LVFX may include the processes of piperazine ring oxidation, quinolone moieties transformation and decarboxylation [54,55]. Combining with the identified intermediate products, three possible photocatalytic degradation pathways of LVFX are put forward in our study. As shown in Fig. 9 , in Path I, under the attack of reactive groups, the piperazine ring is oxidized and further broken to form P1. Some of partially de-alkylated intermediates can be further oxidized into aldehyde products P2-1, P2-2 and P3 [56]. Ultimately, P4 is generated via decarboxylation. Path II mainly refers to the destruction of quinolone moieties. Originally, the F group in LVFX is substituted with a hydroxyl group. And due to the strong oxidizing ability of the reactive groups, the quinolone ring can be easily destroyed and produce the compound P6 and P7 [2]. The third reaction mechanism is demethylation of LVFX to form P8, and it can be further decarboxylation, defluorination and hydroxylation to form P10 [57]. All these intermediates might be further broken into small molecule organic acids as well as other inorganic compounds.

Moreover, three-dimensional excitation-emission matrix fluorescence spectra (3D EEMs) technology was applied to make a deep understanding for the LVFX degradation process. LVFX, as one of fluoroquinolones (FQs), can generate fluorescence signal because of the conjugated heterocyclic structure. From Fig. 10 a, the two evident fluorescence peaks at Ex/Em = 250–300/400–550 nm and Ex/Em = 300–375/400–550 nm referring to humic acid–like substances’ fluorescence region can be detected, which is consistent with previous report [58]. Upon being illuminated by visible light, the fluorescence signal decreases gradually, suggesting the conjugated heterocyclic structure is broken. At the irradiation time of 90 min (Fig. 10d), there are no obvious fluorescence signals, which is attributed to the further destruction of conjugated heterocyclic structure of LVFX. The results of 3D EEMs are consistent with the above LC-MS analysis, which further demonstrates the decomposition of LVFX.

In addition, the mineralization ability of ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 was also investigated to evaluate the performance of the catalyst. Fig. S4 shows the total organic carbon (TOC) remove rate of ZnFe2O4, Ag2CO3 and ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3. Generally speaking, the mineralization activity is weaker than the degradation, which may be ascribed to the formation of intermediate products during the degradation process. As the illumination time is extended to 150 min, mineralization rate further increases, suggesting the intermediate product can be further mineralized. Among the three samples, the prepared ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 composite shows the highest mineralization ratio and approximately 44.75% of the TOC in the LVFX solution is removed, which indicates that the ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 nanocomposite can not only degrade LVFX but also can effectively mineralize LVFX molecules.

In order to evaluate the recyclability and stability of ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 nanocomposite, recycle experiment for degradation of LVFX under the same conditions were conducted by taking advantage of the magnetic property of the nanocomposite. As illustrated in Fig. 11 a, the photocatalytic performance of ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 shows no obvious deactivation after four photodegradation cycles and the degradation efficiency still maintains at about 82.56%, revealing that the photocatalyst possesses a high stability. To further explore the structure changes of ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 sample during cycle experiments, XRD and TEM of used ternary catalyst were carried out. It can be seen from XRD pattern (Fig. 11b) that there is no significant change in crystal structure after four cycles. And it is observed from the TEM image (Fig. S5) that the morphology and microstructure of the used ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 sample remains the same as the fresh one. Besides, transient photocurrent response analysis of used ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 sample was applied to investigate the charges separation and recombination processes. It’s clear in Fig. 11c, the photocurrent response of used ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 is still strong enough and exhibits no obvious decrease after four photodegradation cycles, which suggests the remarkable stability of the prepared photocatalyst. What’s more, the magnetic properties of ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 sample was depicted in Fig. 11d. Within the magnetic field range of − 20000 to 20,000 Oe, the ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 composite presents superparamagnetic behavior with a saturation magnetization value of approximately 5.72 emu g−1, which is strong enough to achieve efficient separation in a short time (inset in Fig. 11d). Consequently, all the results above prove that ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 composite holds excellent long-term stability and reusability for LVFX degradation and has a great potential in practical application.

To gain insight into the trend of photocatalytic performance, a series of characterizations were used to research the behaviors of charge separation and transfer. The photoelectrochemical (PC) analysis is a powerful technique to study the separation and recombination processes of photoexcited charges. Fig. 12 a exhibits the transient photocurrent responses of ZnFe2O4, ZnFe2O4/Ag2CO3 and ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 under intermittent illumination for five on-off cycles. Notably, ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 shows the highest transient photocurrent intensity among all the samples, implying the prepared ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 nanocomposite possesses the highest separation rate of photoexcited charges. This phenomenon may be related to the synergistic effect of the formed Z-scheme transfer pathway and NCDs acting as the charge transfer medium between ZnFe2O4 and Ag2CO3. The promoted charge transfer performance of ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 was further validated by EIS measurement. It is known to us that the semicircular diameter of the EIS Nyquist diagram is related to charge-transfer resistance. Generally speaking, a bigger radius implies a higher charge-transfer resistance. From Fig. 12b, it is obvious that the ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 sample has the smallest semicircle among three composites, indicating that an effective interfacial charge transfer process is achieved. This result is in good accordance with the analysis of the transient photocurrent experiment.

In addition, TRPL decay spectra of pure ZnFe2O4, binary ZnFe2O4/Ag2CO3 and ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 were carried out to investigation the charge carrier lifetime. As shown in Fig. 12c, the average lifespan of ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 is measured to be 0.8057 ns, which is longer than ZnFe2O4/Ag2CO3 (0.6553 ns) and ZnFe2O4 (0.5571 ns). That means an effective electron transport and charge separation process are achieved [7,59]. Moreover, in the Bode-phase spectra (Fig. 12d), the gradually lowered frequency peak of ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 further conforms the prolonged electron lifetime, which is related to enhanced interface charge separation [24]. In brief, all above analysis demonstrate that the introduction of Ag2CO3 and NCDs can obviously improve the charge transfer and separation in ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 photocatalytic system, thus promote the photocatalytic performance.

It is known that a highly efficient photocatalytic system is inseparable from the generation of reactive groups. For investigating the predominate active species for LVFX degradation in photocatalytic process, the radical trapping experiments were carried out by using BQ, IPA and Na2C2O4 as scavenger for
·
O 2 -
, OH and h+, respectively. From Fig. 13 a, we can learn that after putting BQ, IPA and Na2C2O4 into photocatalytic reaction system, the photoactivity of ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 composite is inhibited obviously and the degradation efficiencies decrease to 44.46%, 69.98% and 55.82%, respectively. This result indicates
·
O 2 -
, OH and h+ are all produced and participated in the degradation of LVFX.

For further proving the generation of active species in this photocatalytic system, ESR spintrap with DMPO technique under visible light irradiation was also conducted on ZnFe2O4/Ag2CO3 and ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 catalyst. Fig. 13c shows that there is no apparent signal for DMPO-
·
O 2 -
in the darkness. However, the characteristic peaks of DMPO-
·
O 2 -
are detected in the two composites when illuminated under visible light. Additionally, four obvious peaks of DMPO-OH are also measured when exposed to visible light. And the peak intensity of ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 composites is stronger than the binary one, indicating the promoted
·
O 2 -
and OH generation ability of ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3. Combining with the previous characterization and experiment, the superior
·
O 2 -
and OH generation ability could be attributed to the broadened optical absorption and enhanced charge separation after the introduction of NCDs. The results reveal that both
·
O 2 -
and OH are generated in the photocatalytic process, that is in line with analysis recorded in free radical trapping experiments. Consequently, fom the radicals trap experiments and ESR analysis, we can know that the photocatalytic system is powered by the synergetic effect of OH, h+ and
·
O 2 -
.

In addition, the Mott-Schottky (M-S) test was used to calculate the flat band potential (Efb) of single ZnFe2O4 and Ag2CO3. Generally speaking, the flat potential (Efb) can be calculated through the equation [60]:
1
C
2
=
2
q ε
ε 0
N d
E -
E
f b
-
k b
T
q
where ε0 and ε represent the electric permittivity of the vacuum and the dielectric constant of the used sample, and Nd, C, Kb, T, E, q denote donor density, space charge capacitance, Boltzmann’s constant, Kelvin temperature, applied potential and electronic charge, respectively. As shown in Fig. 14 , the Efb of ZnFe2O4 and Ag2CO3 are measured to be − 0.91 and 0.25 eV (vs. Ag/AgCl), corresponding to −0.71 and 0.45 eV (vs. NHE). What’s more, the positive slopes of the M-S plots indicate the as-prepared ZnFe2O4 and Ag2CO3 are both n-type semiconductors. As a matter of fact, it’s recorded that the Efb potential of an n-type semiconductor is lower than the potential of conduction band position (ECB) by 0.1 eV [61]. Hence, the ECB of pristine ZnFe2O4 and Ag2CO3 are identified as −0.81 and 0.35 eV. Since the band gap energies (Eg) of ZnFe2O4 and Ag2CO3 are measured to be 1.87 and 2.57 eV corresponding to the results of DRS, their valence band (EVB) potential can be estimated to be 1.06 and 2.92 eV by using the following formula as: EVB = ECB + Eg.

Based on above characterization analysis and discussion, a possible Z-scheme photocatalytic mechanism of NCDs and Ag2CO3 co-modified ZnFe2O4 ternary photocatalyst was put forward and illustrated in Scheme 1 . Upon exposure to visible light, both of ZnFe2O4 and Ag2CO3 are able to be excited to produce photoinduced charges because of their narrow bandgaps. Simultaneously, NCDs can act as charge transport mediator between ZnFe2O4 and Ag2CO3. The photogenerated electrons from the CB of Ag2CO3 will transfer to the surface of NCDs and then integrate with the holes in VB of ZnFe2O4, leaving the electrons in CB of ZnFe2O4 and holes in VB of Ag2CO3 accumulated. Moreover, according to the analysis of M-S measurement, the CB potential of ZnFe2O4 (−0.81 eV) is more negative than the reduction potential of O2/
·
O 2 -
(−0.33 eV/vs. NHE) and the VB potential of Ag2CO3 (2.92 eV) is more positive than the potential of OH−/OH (+2.40 eV/vs. NHE), so the photoexcited holes in VB of Ag2CO3 can react with H2O to generate OH while electrons in the CB of ZnFe2O4 are able to reduce O2 into
·
O 2 -
. Consequently, more active groups are generated, which are responsible for the high degradation efficiency of LVFX molecules. It is worth noting that NCDs become an important and indispensable part in the photocatalytic reaction system for the following reasons: (i) NCDs can serve as the charge transfer bridge between ZnFe2O4 and Ag2CO3 due to their remarkable electrical conductivity, which facilitates the recombination of holes from VB of ZnFe2O4 and electrons from CB of Ag2CO3, thereby favoring effective charge separation as well as higher photocatalytic performance. (ii) As a photosensitizer, NCDs bring wider light absorption for the ternary catalytic system owing to their unique upconversion photoluminescence properties, therefore more available charge carriers are formed by the excited ZnFe2O4 and Ag2CO3 and a boosted photocatalytic activity is achieved. (iii) NCDs are able to accept the redundant electrons from ZnFe2O4 and Ag2CO3, which is another method to advance the separation of photoinduced charges. Moreover, the electrons transferred from ZnFe2O4 possess a high reduction energy and can reduce O2 to produce more
·
O 2 -
active species.

In general, the magnetically separable ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 all-solid-state Z-scheme nanocomposite was synthesized by an easy-operated deposition-precipitation route. The obtained ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 nanocomposite exhibits an enhanced photocatalytic activity for LVFX removal, achieving 88.75% and 22.53% degradation efficiency under visible and near-infrared (NIR) light irradiation, respectively. The promoted photocatalytic performance of the as-prepared ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 nanocomposite is attributed to the high separation rate of photoinduced charges as well as wider optical absorption, resulting from the construction of Z-scheme heterojunction and introduction of NCDs. In addition, the results of radical trapping experiments and ESR technology confirm that
·
O 2 -
, OH and h+ are all produced in the photocatalytic process and
·
O 2 -
plays major role for LVFX degradation among three active groups. Furthermore, the proposed ZnFe2O4/NCDs/Ag2CO3 hybrid composite can be recovered and shows remarkable photostability and reusability. This research provides a novel insight for the fabrication of all-solid-state Z-scheme photocatalyst based on nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots and opens new vista for designing the magnetically separable and highly efficient photocatalysts for application in wastewater remediation.
